case_name: State of New Hampshire v. Justin Sales

segments.text: This is case 2024-0239, the state of New Hampshire versus of Justin Sales.
Good morning, Attorney Barnard. I understand you reserved one minute.
I have. Thank you. Good morning. My name is Thomas Barnard. I represent Justin Sales.
I plan to address arguments 1, 3A, and 3C this morning.
And to make three points. First, the evidence was insufficient to support the charged variant of AFSA
because AA was not confined at the time of the sexual activity.
Second, AA's earlier confinement was merely incidental to the physical assaults
and thus did not justify a separate conviction.
And third, Sales' conduct supports only one witness tampering conviction
because he attempted to influence the testimony of one witness in one proceeding.
RSA 632A21E requires that the confinement be concurrent with the sexual activity.
It prohibits sexual activity when the victim submits under circumstances
involving false imprisonment, kidnapping, or extortion.
Here, the confinement had ended at the time the sexual activity took place.
AA testified that both she and Sales had started to chill out,
that Sales apologized, said he didn't mean to hurt her,
and that AA told Sales that she wanted to lay down and cuddle.
So what does the circumstances involving mean if not the broader circumstances of the assault?
It means that the door is locked. It means that the defendant is blocking the exit.
It means that the victim is somehow physically restrained,
or it means that the defendant...
So the legislature, instead of saying circumstances involving,
the legislature could have said wouldn't false imprisonment or confinement occurs?
I don't think... Yeah, I don't think there's...
I don't perceive a difference in those two phrasings.
I think that the phrasing that the legislature shows
indicates that the confinement has to be concurrent with the sexual activity.
It doesn't have to be a physical blocking of the exit.
It could be the defendant saying,
look, if you try to leave, I'm going to punch you.
That would be confinement also.
But there has to be some contemporaneous conduct or statement
that gives rise to that confinement, that constitutes that confinement.
Isn't there evidence though that every time she tried to separate herself
from the defendant that he began to choke her?
During the earlier physical assault, yes.
But isn't that evidence that when she attempted to get away
that he restricted that movement and violently assaulted her?
Why wouldn't she consider, believe that that would happen again
if she tried to get out of the room, leave the bathroom?
Well, we've got this break, this clear break
between the earlier assault and the sexual activity.
And it doesn't turn on her subjective belief.
But the question is whether objectively there was confinement
at the time that sexual activity took place.
And there was, here we have physical assaults
and then we have a resolution.
We have a...
Do we know how much time passed between the last assault
and the incident in the bathroom?
No.
No, we don't.
I can see that it was probably just minutes.
It might not have been a long time.
But that doesn't change the fact
that there was a clear change in the tenor of their interaction.
We have moved from this conflict to reconciliation.
And that reconciliation, we may consider that reconciliation
distasteful, but that's the facts
of what was happening in that apartment at that time.
But isn't that an awfully mechanical application
of the rule of law where sexual activity
or where the defendant creates a state of mind
of terror for the victim?
And just minutes later, are you saying that objectively speaking,
we could not conclude that she remained so afraid
for her safety that she was essentially intimidated
from attempting to escape?
Well, I'm saying that objectively there was no confinement
when the sexual activity took place.
Can't intimidation result in confinement?
It can, certainly it can.
And if we didn't have this change in the dynamics
of their interaction, then I would agree
she could still be confined because of what happened earlier.
But here we have this clear break
in the dynamics of how they're interacting.
We have this movement from conflict to reconciliation.
And that, and it's because of that clear break
that the confinement, any confinement that existed
during the physical assaults, no longer existed
when the sexual activity took place.
So you're really arguing that the state should have charged
them with a different variant of the AFSA structure?
If the state had charged a different variant,
the evidence here might have been sufficient.
There are variants that cover sexual activity
that take place when the actor...
Following threats.
Yeah, following threats.
When the actor threatens to use physical violence
or superior physical strength on the victim,
when the actor coerces the victim
to submit by threatening retaliation,
and this court has held that those threats
can be implied from prior circumstances.
Moving on to argument 3A,
AA's earlier confinement was merely incidental
to the physical assaults.
They did not justify a separate conviction.
AA testified that she was unable to flee
simply because sales was assaulting her.
She did not testify, for instance,
that sales blocked the door.
As in Miller, the confinement here
was incidental and inseparable
from the conduct constituting the assaults.
It was, quote, solely intended to enable sales
to accomplish his overall criminal scheme.
Under this court's merger doctrine,
because the confinement was merely incidental
to the physical assaults, sales could certainly
be convicted of the physical assaults,
but he could not, in addition,
be convicted of confinement as well
and sentenced on that confinement charge.
Turning to argument 3C,
sales' conduct supports only one
witness tampering conviction
because he attempted to influence the testimony
of one witness in one proceeding.
RSA 641.5 prohibits attempting to cause
a person to perform certain acts or omissions
affecting an official proceeding or investigation.
So the gravamen of that offense,
the unit of prosecution,
is each person and each proceeding
or investigation.
The state's argument here
that the unit of prosecution
is each attempt leads to absurd results.
Sales called or attempted to call
AA over 1600 times.
Under the state's argument,
he could be charged and sentenced,
he could be convicted over 1600 times
for witness tampering,
and he could be sentenced to a total sentence
that exceeds 10,000 years.
It is absurd to suggest that the legislature
intended that result
when it enacted the witness tampering statute.
This court should follow
the Supreme Court's opinion and haul,
hold that multiple attempts
to influence a single witness
in a single proceeding constitute
one unit of witness tampering.
What if there were different mechanisms
to engage in that witness tampering?
Phone calls versus text messages
versus written letters,
something like that.
I still think it's still one unit of prosecution
because it's one witness and one proceeding.
The statute covers
all attempts to cause a person
to testify falsely or withhold information.
It doesn't distinguish among different
modes of communication,
so I don't think there's a basis for saying
that each mode of communication constitutes
a unit of prosecution.
Wouldn't it be more logical
if it applied to separate charges,
for example, of calling her on day one
and asking her to testify
that she consented to sexual activity?
On day three, he asked her
to testify that she fell down
and that's how she got injured,
that he didn't assault her.
Doesn't that make more sense?
I can see two potential
basis for dividing up the charge.
One is that it happens on different days.
I don't think there's a basis in the statute
for saying that each day is the unit of prosecution.
A day is just an arbitrary time period.
Why a day instead of a week?
Why a day instead of an hour?
In terms of different requests,
testify to lie number A on one request.
About charge number B
and lie about something else
regarding charge number C.
Right, right.
I don't think there's a basis in the statute
to say that each charge is a...
can be a unit of prosecution.
The statute applies to official proceedings
or investigations.
If there's an investigation,
we have no charges at all.
But we wouldn't say,
well, he can't be convicted of witness tampering
because there are zero charges pending.
So the fact that there are ten charges pending
doesn't mean that the defendant
can be convicted of ten counts of witness tampering
in a way that might just reward the state
for overcharging to begin with.
What about witness tampering that escalates?
So it starts with a phone call
and next it's a threat.
If you don't recant, I'll break your leg.
And the third time,
there's an incident of some kind of physical assault.
Are you saying that all of those would be one unit?
I think there are different variants
of witness tampering.
A person commits witness tampering
if they attempt to influence
a person to testify or inform falsely
or withhold any testimony information document
or thing, that's what was charged here.
I believe there's also a variant of witness tampering
that covers crimes that are committed in retaliation
for a witness providing information or testimony.
And that's a separate variant of witness tampering
and I think in that case the state might have an argument,
a much better argument that that is a separate offense
and the defendant can be convicted multiple times.
If the court has no further questions.
What about the contempt and the court's authority
to order no contact in a bail order
as a condition of release?
So the court certainly has
I agree the court probably has inherent authority
to prohibit a defendant from contacting a witness
even if the defendant is detained.
But that authority does not derive from the bail statute.
It does not derive from RSA 597.
That authority is an exercise
of the court's inherent authority.
And when the court exercises its inherent authority
it has to do so on a,
has to be very circumspect about it
and very restrained about it.
It has to consider questions like
is there a compelling justification for this?
Is there no other reasonable alternative?
Here there is no indication that the court's order
was anything other than a run of the mill bail order
under RSA 597.
There's no indication that the court
acted in that circumspect or restrained way
or considered those questions of compelling justification
or alternatives.
There's simply no basis to look at the order here
and conclude that this was an exercise
of the court's inherent authority.
And in fact the order called the no contact provision
a condition.
Well if it's a condition it has to be a condition of something.
That makes sense if the defendant is released.
The condition of the release is that the defendant
have no contact.
If he does have contact his release can be revoked.
If the defendant is not released then it is illogical
to call the no contact provision a condition
because it can't logically be the condition of anything.
So although the court may have had
your position is although the court may have had
inherent authority to order this under certain conditions
the fact that it was ordered under 597
essentially renders it void.
Is the order just void an issue?
There's no authority for it?
There's no authority under RSA 597.
It would come into force if the court
modified its order and said
I'm changing preventative detention
to detention on $100,000 cash or surety
and the defendant were released
then that provision would come into force.
But as long as sales was detained
the condition of release did not apply.
He was not released.
Thank you.
Good morning and may it please the court
Adriana McCoola for the state of New Hampshire.
I want to start with something that you asked
Justice Gould when we were talking about
whether there was sufficient evidence
of the AFSAs in this case.
It would be a mechanical operation of the statute
that would require that this confinement,
this kidnapping or false imprisonment
or what have you be occurring
simultaneously to the sexual assaults.
That is not the way that the statute is written.
It is written as happening under circumstances
involving false imprisonment or kidnapping
or confinement which the trial court
broadly defined for the jury
as detention that restricts
a person's movement without their consent
and that includes confinement
that is accomplished by force, threat or deception.
The requirement that it be happening simultaneously
or contemporaneously or whatever other word
concurrently I think is the word
that the defendant used today in this argument.
The legislature does know how to write
statutes that do that.
In the capital murder offenses
or in the homicide offenses it does say
if you commit a murder while that person
is being kidnapped that simultaneously
if those two things occur
then that's a heightened penalty.
Here the language, circumstances involving
allows for the jury to look at
the entire criminal episode
whether it be in this case hours or perhaps even an hour
it's unclear how long this assault
really happened for
or over a period of days
because it is certainly possible
in there are cases out there that this court has decided
on other legal issues where the facts have been
that a person was kidnapped
or falsely imprisoned for days of time
and in that span of time
they were sexually assaulted
and that counted as this happening
under circumstances involving false imprisonment.
In this case there was
a plethora of evidence that she was
feeling as if she were confined
when these sexual assaults occurred.
Leading up to her being in the bathroom
she had been sleeping
after the defendant had gone out to the club
they had both been drinking.
She I think wanted to go to sleep instead of going out.
When he comes home he wakes her up
and the first thing he says to her
is to accuse her of cheating on him.
She says leave me alone
and she tries to get out of the bedroom
the living room to get away from him.
When she does that
she hears him break her phone
and then she feels him come up behind her
and begin to strangle her.
She is able to get away from him at that point
and then
when she fights back he strikes her everywhere.
She says on her face, on her legs
on her arms everywhere.
She does have broken ribs.
She has injuries all over her body when she goes to the hospital.
She then gets away
and is able to make it into the kitchen
immediately and then strangles her again.
This time she bites his thumb
hard to make him let go
and he bites her back numerous times.
I think there are multiple charges
for bites on her back and there were pictures
of those bites admitted at trial.
Then after she is able to get away
from him in the kitchen
she describes crawling into the bathroom
so that she could look at her face
wash it
and take a drink of water and figure out a plan
and it is while she is doing that
I think really honestly within
it may even be within seconds
he is in the doorway of that bathroom
while she is kneeling on the ground
after seeing herself bleeding from her nose
and her eyes are all bruised
one I think is swollen shut at this point.
He says, I want you to perform oral sex.
We don't know exactly what he said
that's how she described it at trial.
She says in trial
I think on cross examination even
I did not want to have sex
I did not want, I wanted to leave
before the sex occurred
but I also wanted to leave when he fell asleep
so that I could get my cat and get out safely
so her intention was not
to have sex with him.
She says to him in the bathroom
I just want to lay down in cuddle
after he apologizes in the hopes
that he will fall asleep
without anything else happening to her
so that she can leave.
It's in that moment that he demands
oral sex and she said
on direct that she was scared not to do that
that lasted seconds according to her
and then he demanded to have vaginal sex
with her. She again said I was afraid
not to allow that
that also lasted for seconds
and then he laid down on the bathroom floor
she rubbed his back until he fell asleep
and then she immediately picked up her cat
with nothing else and left.
So there is no doubt I think
for the jury to have found in this case
that she felt confined not only based on
what had happened leading up to those assaults
but her statements on direct
that she was scared not to
also her statements that
Is the victim's subjective belief
really relevant to whether or not
this element has been met?
Isn't it an objective standard?
So I don't know that the court has ever decided
like it has for criminal threatening
for example that you need both a subjective
belief from the victim that they are
really in danger and the subjective belief
that this is a real threat.
I don't know that this court has held that in this context
but I do think both are important
and if the jury did not feel coerced
to submit then it's entirely possible
that the jury would not feel
that this sexual assault had occurred
because she would therefore have consented.
So I do think both are important.
I think objectively the jury had sufficient
evidence to find that she did
really feel scared to not have sex with him
and I think the jury could also
sort of untangle her statements
on cross where she's saying
I freely chose to have sex with him
but I was scared to do so
on cross examination when the defense attorney
asked her to explain or reconcile
those two statements for the jury
she said if I hadn't been strangled
and beaten before it would be a different
story but I didn't know what would
happen if I had said no and so even
though she's trying to say to the jury
I made this free choice it's very clear
from her own statements and feelings
about that moment in the bathroom
that she did not feel that it was safe
to say no and that is
the coercion and the sexual assault
being under circumstances involving
confinement.
If there are no further questions on that point
I would move to the second argument
about the court's inherent authority
to issue a bail order
or a no contact provision even when
there's detainment.
Can you get to that? Sure.
What's the significance
in your brief on pages
16 and 17 you make reference
to a separate protective order? Yes.
And what effect
does that protective order have
on the issue of
sufficiency on the stocking
and contempt charges?
So I believe I'd have to look back at the charges.
I think the charges
for stocking and contempt
were based on the bail order that was
issued under 597.2 which I know
there's a copy. The other
protective order is a criminal bail protective
order that was issued
that also included a no contact order
and while I don't know that it was relevant
to sufficiency in terms of the jury didn't
receive that criminal bail protective order
that's not what the state relied on to prove
that order existed. However
if we're looking at the court's inherent
authority to issue no contact orders to
detained individuals at the bail hearing
which is only a 14 page transcript
the trial court after it ordered the defendant
detained told him
another condition is that you
not commit any crimes and that you also
not have contact with the victim
if released the court then listed several
conditions and then she also said
separate from the release
you also have this
it's called a criminal bail protective order
I'm not sure what the judge called it at the hearing
she may have called it a restraining order
or something else but this also requires
that you not have contact with the victim
in the same way that I've described as part
of this bail order that I'm issuing
and you have to comply with that as well
and the court certainly has statutory
authority and inherent authority to issue
those criminal bail protective orders
that I've met of which this case is
one and that requires
no contact regardless of release
the state didn't charge under
that criminal bail protective order
however the court certainly had
authority I think under both 597
2 and its inherent
authority to craft a bail
order that not only protected
the community from the defendant and
perhaps protected the defendant from himself
if you were a danger to himself
but also protected
the court's ability to administer justice
in a fair way
and ensure that both parties could come
to this court and come to this trial
and present their case fairly
I think those are intertwined
I think the set 597
2 does not say courts cannot
issue conditions for detained people
that statute does list out
the court's ability to
have some sort of combination of conditions
that would protect the community
and protect the defendant
and the court and the statute rather also
does not limit what those conditions are
while there are certainly conditions on the form
the court form that this court approved
there's also another section
where a court can add whatever conditions it wants
and even on
the first page of this bail order
it says above
where it says if released
it says the defendant is subject to the following
additional conditions and above that if released
the trial court listed this no contact
order so I think it's clear
that the court was using its authority
under the bail statute and its inherent
authority to issue this no contact order
to the defendant in a multitude
of ways and so it was very clear
he knew he could not contact her
while he was detained
if there are no further questions on that
issue I will move to the last issue
about merger
I'd like to start with the witness tampering
but I'm happy to start with criminal restrain
if you prefer the unit of prosecution
in a witness tampering case
those each attempt that a defendant makes
and it's based on
the specific words he's using
and I would direct this court to an unpublished opinion
from 2024
state v. Rivera where this court
in a very similar fashion it was
under a plan error this merger argument
was not raised down below this court
reviewed the issue of whether the defendants
for witness tampering convictions
could stand as they were
or if some of them had to merge
and this court found that two of them
were fine based on the calls
that were made but that two others
those charges were specifically
from the exact same phrases
that the defendant had used
I don't exactly remember the quote it's in the case
and I have the site
that I can grab in one second if anyone needs it
but it was essentially the same
four lines of statement that the defendant
had made through a jail call to another person
and the state had charged two variants
of witness tampering from that same statement
and this court said you can't
sentence him to two separate witness tampering
convictions for the same exact words
that are used and while this court did not
say in that unpublished case
that means then that the words are the unit of
prosecution I think that's what this court
was driving at that it's the words
that the defendant is using whether
they come in one phone call or 1600
phone calls that are
the unit of prosecution because
the state has to prove a purposeful
mens rea it is inaccurate to say
that the state could bring and prove a trial
1600 witness tampering charges
because not all of those
1600 phone calls were answered
so we have no idea what he intended
to say to her.
There are plenty of calls he made that
presumably no witness tampering occurred
not only based on the fact that there are only 19 charges
but in the sentencing transcript
the judge talked about how it was very clear
from the pendant the way the case had played out
that the victim and the defendant
were very much in love while this case
was pending and so it's very possible that some
of those calls were not witness tampering
they certainly maybe violated the no
contact order and perhaps the state could have
brought 1600 stocking
or contempt charges
but not witness tampering because they're based
on the words that are being used
and that's how these charges are
brought here I don't have all 19
out for you but some of them talk about
the defendant telling her to go in and say she has
amnesia some of them say
go make this false statement about
kinky sex gone wrong some of them
say you need to get me out on bail
because I need to be with my son
so he's using all different methods
of words and phrases
and motivations
on his part to witness tamper her
and each one of those statements is
the unit of prosecution
moving to the
criminal restraint argument
that does not merge
in this case with the simple assaults
the victim testified
at the trial in addition
to all of the statements she made about
being scared not to give him what he wanted
she had said that
she when she had tried to leave
as you pointed out just as Donovan he had pulled her back numerous times
and she also said
that she feared further harm
to herself if she left while he was
awake and it's that fear
of future harm that separates
this criminal restraint and prevents it from
being merged with these other assaults
how do you distinguish this case from Miller
so in Miller
that case where
the way the charges for false imprisonment
and unprivileged physical contact were brought
by the state where they both
charge the exact same actions by the defendant
which was pinning the victim down
by putting his hand on her neck
the state used that pinning down
as unprivileged physical contact
and as the false imprisonment
here the way the state charged this criminal
restraint it was that
she was confined by the defendant under
circumstances involving a risk
of serious bodily injury
that is separate from all of the serious bodily injury
that she very much did experience
during that assault because she testified
at least twice at this trial
that she was afraid of what would happen to her
if she left while the defendant was awake
and her
attempts to escape and his
pulling her back into the apartment
so those combined mean that
this is a not incidental to these assaults
he did not have to criminally
restrain her and that was not his only purpose
in committing these assaults
it was a separate offense on which
he can be sentenced at this juncture
if there are
I'm happy to address the one argument

thank you
thank you
thank you I just want to address briefly the

the indictment

it's the same exact allegation

the second degree assault and the domestic violence charges

thank you

initial_prompt: This is a New Hampshire Supreme Court oral argument. Preserve legal terminology, party names, docket numbers, and court names. Likely speakers include justices and counsel.
text:
This is case 2024-0239, the state of New Hampshire versus of Justin Sales.

Good morning, Attorney Barnard. I understand you reserved one minute.

I have. Thank you. Good morning. My name is Thomas Barnard. I represent Justin Sales.

I plan to address arguments 1, 3A, and 3C this morning.

And to make three points. First, the evidence was insufficient to support the charged variant of AFSA

because AA was not confined at the time of the sexual activity.

Second, AA's earlier confinement was merely incidental to the physical assaults

and thus did not justify a separate conviction.

And third, Sales' conduct supports only one witness tampering conviction

because he attempted to influence the testimony of one witness in one proceeding.

RSA 632A21E requires that the confinement be concurrent with the sexual activity.

It prohibits sexual activity when the victim submits under circumstances

involving false imprisonment, kidnapping, or extortion.

Here, the confinement had ended at the time the sexual activity took place.

AA testified that both she and Sales had started to chill out,

that Sales apologized, said he didn't mean to hurt her,

and that AA told Sales that she wanted to lay down and cuddle.

So what does the circumstances involving mean if not the broader circumstances of the assault?

It means that the door is locked. It means that the defendant is blocking the exit.

It means that the victim is somehow physically restrained,

or it means that the defendant...

So the legislature, instead of saying circumstances involving,

the legislature could have said wouldn't false imprisonment or confinement occurs?

I don't think... Yeah, I don't think there's...

I don't perceive a difference in those two phrasings.

I think that the phrasing that the legislature shows

indicates that the confinement has to be concurrent with the sexual activity.

It doesn't have to be a physical blocking of the exit.

It could be the defendant saying,

look, if you try to leave, I'm going to punch you.

That would be confinement also.

But there has to be some contemporaneous conduct or statement

that gives rise to that confinement, that constitutes that confinement.

Isn't there evidence though that every time she tried to separate herself

from the defendant that he began to choke her?

During the earlier physical assault, yes.

But isn't that evidence that when she attempted to get away

that he restricted that movement and violently assaulted her?

Why wouldn't she consider, believe that that would happen again

if she tried to get out of the room, leave the bathroom?

Well, we've got this break, this clear break

between the earlier assault and the sexual activity.

And it doesn't turn on her subjective belief.

But the question is whether objectively there was confinement

at the time that sexual activity took place.

And there was, here we have physical assaults

and then we have a resolution.

We have a...

Do we know how much time passed between the last assault

and the incident in the bathroom?

No.

No, we don't.

I can see that it was probably just minutes.

It might not have been a long time.

But that doesn't change the fact

that there was a clear change in the tenor of their interaction.

We have moved from this conflict to reconciliation.

And that reconciliation, we may consider that reconciliation

distasteful, but that's the facts

of what was happening in that apartment at that time.

But isn't that an awfully mechanical application

of the rule of law where sexual activity

or where the defendant creates a state of mind

of terror for the victim?

And just minutes later, are you saying that objectively speaking,

we could not conclude that she remained so afraid

for her safety that she was essentially intimidated

from attempting to escape?

Well, I'm saying that objectively there was no confinement

when the sexual activity took place.

Can't intimidation result in confinement?

It can, certainly it can.

And if we didn't have this change in the dynamics

of their interaction, then I would agree

she could still be confined because of what happened earlier.

But here we have this clear break

in the dynamics of how they're interacting.

We have this movement from conflict to reconciliation.

And that, and it's because of that clear break

that the confinement, any confinement that existed

during the physical assaults, no longer existed

when the sexual activity took place.

So you're really arguing that the state should have charged

them with a different variant of the AFSA structure?

If the state had charged a different variant,

the evidence here might have been sufficient.

There are variants that cover sexual activity

that take place when the actor...

Following threats.

Yeah, following threats.

When the actor threatens to use physical violence

or superior physical strength on the victim,

when the actor coerces the victim

to submit by threatening retaliation,

and this court has held that those threats

can be implied from prior circumstances.

Moving on to argument 3A,

AA's earlier confinement was merely incidental

to the physical assaults.

They did not justify a separate conviction.

AA testified that she was unable to flee

simply because sales was assaulting her.

She did not testify, for instance,

that sales blocked the door.

As in Miller, the confinement here

was incidental and inseparable

from the conduct constituting the assaults.

It was, quote, solely intended to enable sales

to accomplish his overall criminal scheme.

Under this court's merger doctrine,

because the confinement was merely incidental
